pratyaksa.gloss: Direct sensory cognition: knowledge arising from contact between sense-organ and object.

anumana.gloss: Knowledge of an unperceived object via a known invariable concomitance (vyāpti) with a perceived sign (liṅga).

sabda.gloss: Knowledge from a reliable utterance, paradigmatically apauruṣeya śruti for the Vedāntin.

upamana.gloss: Knowledge of a thing's resemblance to a previously cognized object, accepted as an independent pramāṇa by Mīmāṃsā and Advaita epistemology.

arthapatti.gloss: Knowledge by necessary supposition: the only explanation of an otherwise inexplicable fact (e.g., Devadatta is fat though he never eats by day, so he must eat by night).

anupalabdhi.gloss: Cognition of absence: knowledge of a thing's non-existence in a locus by the failure of a competent perception that would have apprehended it had it been present.

smrti.gloss: Authoritative tradition derivative of (and consistent with) śruti; for the Vedāntin a derivative source of right cognition.

sambhava.gloss: Knowledge of a part-quantity from cognition of the larger whole that includes it (e.g., one hundred is in one thousand).

abhava.gloss: When treated as a distinct pramāṇa (Bhāṭṭa Mīmāṃsā), the cognitive instrument for knowing absence; otherwise subsumed under anupalabdhi.
